title: Dawgs Of War
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Comes the British bulldog first, solid as a log,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s so ugly in repose that heן¿½s a handsome dog;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Full of mild benevolence as his years increase;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Silent as a china dog on the mantelpiece.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rub his sides and point his nose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Click your tongue and in he goes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the thick of Britainן¿½s foes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Enemies behind him close,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Silence for a while.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Comes a very different dog, tell him at a glance.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Clipped and trimmed and frilled all round. Dandy dog of France.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Always was a dandy dog, no matter what his age)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now his every hair and frill is stiff as wire with rage.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rub his sides and point his nose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Click your tongue and in he goes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While behind him Franceן¿½s foes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Reel and surge and pack and close.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Silence for a while.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Next comes Belgiumן¿½s market dog, hard to realise.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Go-cart dog and barrow dog, heן¿½s a great surprise.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dog that never hurt a cat, did no person harm;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Friendly, kindly, round and fat as a ן¿½Johnny Darm.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rub his sides and point his nose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Click your tongue and in he goes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the flank of Belgiumן¿½s foes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who could not behind him close,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Silence for a while.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Next comes Serviaן¿½s mongrel pup, mongrel dawgs can fight;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Up or down, or down or up, whether wrong or right.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was mad the other day, he is mad today,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hustling round and raising dust in his backyard way.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rub his sides and point his nose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Click your tongue and in he goes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twixt the legs of Serviaן¿½s foes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Biting tails and rearmost toes,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Silence for a while.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are various terrier dawgs mixed up in the scrap,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Much too small for us to see, and too mad to yap.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each one, on his frantic own, heard the row commence,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tore with tooth and claw a hole in the backyard fence.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No one called, but in they go,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dogs with many a nameless woe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tripping up their common foe,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Silence for a while.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the snows of Canada, dragging box and bale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Comes the sledge-dog toiling on, sore-foot from the trail.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½ll be useful in the trench, when the nose is blue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Winter dog that knows the French and the English too.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rub his sides and point his nose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Click your tongue and in he goes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At his fatherן¿½s countryן¿½s foes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his motherן¿½s countryן¿½s foes.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Silence for a while.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ See, in sunny Southern France a dog that runs by sight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lean and yellow, sharp of nose, long of leg and light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Silent and bloodthirsty, too; Distance in his eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leaping high to gain his view, the Kangaroo Dog flies!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rub his sides and point his nose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Click your tongue and up he goes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lands amongst his countryן¿½s foes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his countryן¿½s countryן¿½s foes;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While they sway and while they close,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Silence for a while.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ See across the early snow, far across the plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the clouds are grey and low and winter comes again;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the sand-dune and the marsh, and forest black and dumb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As dusky white as their winterן¿½s night, the Russian wolf-hounds come!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Silence for a while.)
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
